abstract: This study utilizes pulsed field gradient (PFG) NMR spectroscopy to investigate the self-diffusion of molecular and ionic components in aqueous mixtures of choline chloride-based deep eutectic solvents (DESs). By examining Ethaline, Glyceline, and Reline, the research clarifies how varying water concentrations influence both macroscopic transport properties and microscopic intermolecular interactions. The results demonstrate that the addition of water significantly alters the diffusion behavior of hydroxyl protons. In aqueous Ethaline, the hydroxyl species of both the choline cation and the hydrogen bond donor (HBD) exhibit enhanced interactions with water as hydration increases. In contrast, Glyceline maintains relatively stable hydroxyl proton dynamics despite water addition. Notably, in Reline, the presence of water appears to facilitate the localized formation of ammonium hydroxide. A significant finding across the systems is that water often diffuses faster than predicted for a homogeneous mixture, suggesting that these liquids undergo microscopic phase separation even when components are nominally hydrophilic. These insights underscore the utility of PFG NMR in deconstructing the complex competitive interactions and structural inhomogeneities that define the behavior of aqueous DES systems.
